questions.prompt: Which of these is the clearest example of *non-commodity* content?

choices.text: '7 Tips for First-Time Homebuyers' — generic advice any agent could write.
'Why We Waived the Inspection & Saved Money: A Look Inside the Sewer Line' — first-hand story with specific outcomes from one buyer's actual experience.
'Complete guide to home buying' — comprehensive overview piece.

questions.explanation: Google literally uses these two examples in the AI Optimization Guide. The 'Why We Waived the Inspection' piece is non-commodity because it draws on first-hand experience with specific outcomes — information that wouldn't exist without that specific buyer's story. The other options are all commodity: information widely available, could have been written by anyone.

questions.prompt: Your B2B blog has 800 commodity-style posts (e.g., 'What is X', 'Best practices for Y'). Traffic is dropping after each core update. Which approach is most aligned with Google's people-first guidance?

choices.text: Add more keyword variations to each post.
Audit each post; cull the ones with no defensible non-commodity angle; rewrite the rest with specific experience, numbers, and a contrarian or surprising perspective.
Publish 1,000 more posts to dilute the impact of the weak ones.
Add AI-generated 'expanded versions' to each post to make them longer.

questions.explanation: Quality cuts and substantive rewrites are the durable fix. Producing more of the same problem doesn't solve the problem — Google's scaled content abuse policy explicitly targets that pattern. Adding length without substance doesn't help either. The rewrite pattern is: specific numbers, first-person framing, contrarian or surprising hook, promise of specific takeaways.
